annotations.comment: I like this title. One giant paragraph is really hard for the reader to take in — to be honest, my heart kind of sinks whenever I see this. Now, I do think there’s an argument that perhaps flash fiction can be deliberately formatted this way for effect but I would use it cautiously.

paragraphs.body: I start typing away at one project, to typing some more for an assignment for another class after I made progress on the prior project, and just after that assignment start grinding my fingers away for another project for a different class. Hours pass by and as my left wrist aches, that might be a bigger problem in the future, I turn to face away from my computer screens and start to question if any of this is necessary. Like a college student who thinks looking somewhat presentable to class is just a fad, or a baby who thinks toilet training isn’t necessary if their mommy will just change their diaper for them. Just like those examples, I too question if this endless pile of work is necessary for a successful future. Maybe it isn’t? I say to myself, well in my brain really, “Am I going to make it?” I stop for a minute, doubting everything I’ve been working up to, and I can’t help but feel stuck; frozen even. It’s as if my life has been on a loop since going into college! I legit start by waking up at 4:30 AM, drink two cups of coffee, pee and brush my teeth, go to college, sit through a class, take notes, wait around campus for about an hour, sit through another class, maybe take some notes, then wait around some more, go to my last class, not take any notes, go home, workout, then do homework, go to the bathroom in between homework several times, and then pass out mid assignment. Every day, Monday through Friday. Sometimes weekends break that trend, I actually have a day off, but even then I just go back to working on something. Work.. That’s all it seems to be! I’m not doing my work right now just to think about work! Not to mention the stress that comes with the work as well. I swear some days I’m balding from the amount of stress doing just one project brings me.. hypothetically speaking. Need to check with my doctor on that, or just change my hair routine. Maybe I’m overthinking again? Anyways, as I’m sitting here at my desk, stressed and depressed from the stress, my ma breaks into my room. Well, she opens the door in this creepy, gentile way, but the creaking from the floorboards usually is a dead give away that she’s entering my room. She notices that I’m not my usual self, a version of me who is less stressed and depressed from the stress, and says something along the lines of, “College getting to you again?” I usually slightly nod my head yes, and my ma hastily follows up with, “Quit thinking about the future and focus on the now.” My ma lightly closes the door, as she always does, then proceeds to do whatever ma does. It’s usually watching her show on missing person cases or murders at an uncomfortably high volume while she folds laundry, or stresses over whatever came in the mail for the day. I think she needs a break. Anyways, after just five more minutes of stressing over this project, I start to actually hear and process my mother’s words. She is right, I need to start focusing on now not then! How else will I have a good future if I don’t? I sharply stand tall, quickly turn to my computer screens, which are now on sleep mode, flex my wrists because they are sore, again, might be a bigger issue, and—

annotations.comment: You do convey the sense of a grind here really well. This phrasing is a little off. Something more like “I start by tying away at one project, then move to typing some more…”

annotations.text: Like a college student who thinks looking somewhat presentable to class is just a fad, or a baby who thinks toilet training isn’t necessary if their mommy will just change their diaper for them. Just like those examples, I too question if this endless pile of work is necessary for a successful future.
annotations.comment: While I liked these examples I didn’t connect them to the question the narrator is posing here.
I would break the paragraph here. You’re capturing the stress of being a student really well.
This made me laugh. You can take notes in my class. :)

annotations.text: I’m not doing my work right now just to think about work!

annotations.comment: Again, I’d break the paragraph here. I don’t see a strong argument for why this needs to be one paragraph.
annotations.context_left: Need to check with my doctor on that, or just change my hair routine. Maybe I’m overthinking again?
annotations.context_right: as I’m sitting here at my desk, stressed and depressed from the stress, my ma breaks into my room. W

annotations.comment: The ending didn’t quite work for me. I liked the idea of it, but I didn’t follow what he did next. Adeline, I enjoyed this short draft. It’s a nice little window into the stress of being a college student and I hope wasn’t too much based on your own experience. You reminded me of how stressful school can be, and how easy it is to lose track of (or even wonder) why you’re doing it. The plot of the story as it is now is a little unclear — you narrator needs a reason to keep going, and then his ma seems to provide one and then — well, as I said above, the ending is not yet clear. I suggest two possible ways of making the plot a little more clear. First, you could try to have this character attempt to take a break/get some rest and keep getting foiled from doing so. He’s pretty much just nose to the grindstone right now and while we see that he’s not happy, we don’t really see him trying to be more happy until the mother intercedes. Alternately, you could have the mother try to intervene throughout the story and then, finally, succeed in getting him to calm down a bit by the end. Or maybe you see another possibility for this plot, but that’s what I’m missing right now — his attempt (or someone else’s attempt) to get off the treadmill and just be, even briefly. Strong work!
